sections.title: What is Energy?
sections.content_summary: The section introduces energy by inviting students to observe a kitchen and notice activities like cooking, movement, light, and sound. It establishes that energy is what makes things move, light up, produce sound, do work, and change temperature. Students explore energy through three hands-on activities: a balloon releasing air (kinetic energy), a rubber band guitar (sound energy), and a sun-powered water warmer (heat energy from the Sun).

tasks.task_text: Take a balloon and blow into it until it fills up. Hold it tightly. Then, release the mouth of the balloon and observe what happens.
What makes these things move, shine, make a sound or get warm and cold?

sections.title: Sources of Energy
sections.content_summary: This section explores food as the primary energy source for humans and animals, using a classroom narrative to connect eating to movement and activity. It extends to fuels — petrol, diesel, cooking gas, wood, and coal — as energy sources for vehicles and cooking, noting that burning wood or coal creates pollution. The IKS element appears here: in Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, and Ladakh, people traditionally keep cattle on the ground floor of houses so animal heat warms the family living above, reducing the need for firewood or electricity. Activity 4 uses diyas (oil lamps) to demonstrate how oil acts as a fuel.

tasks.task_text: How is food cooked in your house?
What kind of fuel do you use at home for cooking?
What are the problems using too much wood or coal?

Place two diyas (lamps) on a flat surface. In Diya 1—place a cotton wick without any oil, and in Diya 2—place a cotton wick and pour some oil around it. Light the wicks in both the diyas under adult supervision. Observe what happens to the diyas. (a) Which diya burns longer? Why? (b) What is acting as a fuel here?

sections.title: Electricity
sections.content_summary: Students learn that electricity powers devices in homes, schools, and industries, enabling movement, light, sound, and heat. The section covers safe use of electricity — not touching wires, avoiding sockets, staying away from fallen poles and transformers. A 'Do you know?' box introduces energy efficiency, citing LED bulbs as an example of using less electricity for the same output. Traditional Indian houses with small windows and thick walls are mentioned as a passive energy-efficient design.

tasks.task_text: Walk around your home or classroom. Identify five things that run on electricity. Fill in the following table with Device, What it does, and What it requires (light/sound/heat/movement/cooling/other).

sections.title: Generating Energy from the Sun, Wind and Water
sections.content_summary: This section introduces clean energy — solar, wind, and water — as pollution-free alternatives to coal-based electricity. Students explore how solar panels, windmills, and fast-moving rivers generate electricity through three construction activities: a paper pinwheel (wind energy), a magnifying-glass sunlight focus experiment (solar heat energy), and a paper-cup water wheel (water energy). Activity 9 is a whole-class Energy Flow Game linking source → type → use. The section notes that Cochin International Airport in Kerala is the world's first airport entirely powered by solar energy, and that traditional gharaats (watermills) in hilly areas use flowing water to grind grains. Ancient Indian traders from Gujarat and Tamil Nadu used wind-powered sails for maritime trade.

tasks.task_text: Can you think of more examples where we use the Sun, the wind or the flowing water?
List actions that you see in the picture above and fill in the following table with Activity and Source of Energy.

Take a square piece of paper. Draw its two diagonals with the help of a ruler. Now, you have four equal parts. Cut halfway along each of the four lines. Fold each corner and pin it to the stick as shown in the image. Now, hold your pinwheel in the wind. If there is no wind, hold it up and run with it. What happens to your pinwheel?
Take a piece of paper and a magnifying glass. Place the paper in direct sunlight and use the magnifying glass to focus the light on it. What happens to your paper?

Take an empty paper cup and five spoons. Make five holes around the cup and insert the spoons. Then, insert a pencil or straw through the bottom of the cup. Observe that the cup moves around the pencil like a wheel. Balance your wheel on a container as shown in the image. Now, pour water onto your wheel. Does the water make the wheel move?

Energy Flow Game: Prepare paper slips with names or pictures of energy sources (the Sun, wind, water, food, fuel and electricity), types of energy (heat, light, movement, sound), and uses (drying clothes, lighting a bulb, turning a turbine, cooking, running, etc.). Each student receives one card. Walk around the class to find the two others who complete your energy chain (source—type—use). Present your matched group to the class.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection section consolidates the chapter's learning through seven questions and creative tasks. Students consider the impact of no electricity, compare clean and dirty energy sources, identify examples of stored energy, and think of personal energy-saving actions. Items 5 and 6 involve observation and data collection. Item 7 asks students to draw a clean energy home plan and maintain a one-day 'energy diary' recording every instance of fuel or electricity use.

tasks.task_text: What will happen if there is no electricity in your house for a day?
Why is it better to use solar or wind energy instead of coal?
Give two examples where you have seen energy being stored.
What is the one thing you can do at home to save energy?
Find out how many kilometres a vehicle travels per litre of petrol or diesel. Ask about different vehicles. How will you compare them?
Look around your home or classroom. List any three objects that use energy and mention their source of energy. For example: Object; Fan → Energy Source: Electricity
Draw or make a simple plan of a 'clean energy home' that uses solar, wind or any such source of energy.
Make 'my energy diary' for one day, record the number of times you have used the electricity fuel and so on.

dual_strand.natural: Energy as a universal phenomenon — its forms (kinetic, heat, sound, light), sources (Sun, food, fuel, wind, water), and how it makes things work
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human use and conservation of energy across contexts — traditional practices like cattle-heated homes (Himachal/Ladakh), gharaats, Vaastu Shastra, wind-powered maritime trade, and the shift to clean energy (solar parks, electric vehicles)
